title: Dog Excessive Drooling When Anxious: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog drooling from anxiety? Learn why this happens, when to worry, and how photos help our veterinary triage team assess your pet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Excessive drooling in anxious dogs is usually a low-urgency stress response that resolves once the stressor is removed. Seek immediate veterinary care if the drooling is accompanied by lethargy, blue gums, or suspected poisoning.

Why does my dog drool excessively when they are anxious?

Excessive drooling, or ptyalism, occurs when a dog's nervous system is overstimulated. When a dog feels anxious or stressed, their body enters a fight-or-flight state, which can trigger the salivary glands to overproduce. This is common in dogs with noise phobias, separation anxiety, or travel sickness.

Is excessive drooling from anxiety a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Generally Low; it is usually a temporary physiological response that resolves once the stressor is removed.
  • Seek immediate care if: Drooling is accompanied by extreme lethargy or blue-tinged gums.
  • Toxic Ingestion: Immediate veterinary attention is required if you suspect your dog has ingested something toxic.

How does taking a photo help the vet triage my dog's drooling?

  • Provides Context: A photo or video allows a professional to see the consistency of the saliva.
  • Behavioral Assessment: Professionals can evaluate the dog's facial tension and overall body language.
  • Differential Diagnosis: Helps differentiate between a behavioral reaction and a potential physical blockage or dental issue.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

While the provided excerpts do not directly address excessive drooling (ptyalism) as a primary symptom of anxiety in dogs, they do discuss anxiety and fear-related behaviors. Anxious dogs may exhibit behaviors such as avoidance, escape, aggression, low body posture, piloerection, vocalization, or displacement behaviors such as yawning or lip licking. If anxiety is suspected, underlying medical conditions, cognitive dysfunction, and potential fear-evoking stimuli should be investigated. Anxiolytic medications like trazodone, clonidine, clonazepam, gabapentin, or buspirone may be considered in refractory cases, but caution is advised due to potential disinhibition and increased aggression. Pheromone therapy, such as Adaptil, may also be used to help reduce anxiety.

Chapter: Behavioral Medicine

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1567)

faqs.question: How can I stop my dog from drooling when they are nervous?
faqs.answer: The best way to stop the drooling is to address the underlying anxiety through desensitization training, calming pheromones, or prescribed anxiety medications from your veterinarian.

faqs.question: Could the drooling be caused by something other than anxiety?
faqs.answer: Yes, drooling can also be caused by dental disease, nausea, heatstroke, or oral injuries. If the drooling occurs when your dog is relaxed, it warrants a physical exam.

faqs.question: How long does it take for the drooling to stop?
faqs.answer: Typically, once the dog is removed from the stressful environment and begins to calm down, the excessive salivation should subside within 15 to 30 minutes.
